title: Generating Training Data with Language Models: Towards Zero-Shot Language Understanding

structured.summary: The paper presents SuperGen, a method for zero-shot learning in NLU using PLMs by synthesizing class-conditioned texts for fine-tuning without task-specific data. SuperGen demonstrates improved performance over zero-shot methods while being competitive with few-shot learning approaches.

weaknesses.evidence: The use of synthetic training data is not new, with similar techniques previously applied using LSTM-based models. The paper should specify its unique aspects. Lines 92-93.
The evaluation is limited to seven classification tasks from GLUE, restricting the scope of applicability. Lines 49-50.
The reliance on fine-tuned hyperparameters like temperature and top-k suggests a need for extensive tuning, possibly reducing practical applicability. Lines 108-109.

weaknesses.point: Unclear Impact of Components on Performance
weaknesses.evidence: The contribution of each component like label smoothing or temporal ensembling lacks detailed evaluation through isolated analyses or ablation studies. Lines 52-54.
The approach is pitched for classification problems but lacks discussion on its applicability to other task types. Lines 100-101.

weaknesses.point: Ambiguity in Data Quality Control Mechanisms
weaknesses.evidence: Criteria for selecting quality generated data remain vague without specified thresholds or methods. Lines 52-53.
The paper focuses on zero-shot prompting comparisons without addressing transfer learning or data augmentation baselines. Lines 49-51.
The claim of performance equivalent to few-shot methods needs stronger evidence across a broader set of metrics. Lines 13-16.
Discussion on potential biases in generated data and their implications in real-world settings is missing.
The process for replicating key results, including text generation and selection, is underexplained, relying on supplementary material instead of the main text.

weaknesses.point: Lack of Task Diversity in Evaluation
weaknesses.evidence: Evaluation solely on classification tasks limits generalizability to other important NLU tasks like sequence labeling or QA.

weaknesses.point: Scalability to Larger Tasks or Corpuses
weaknesses.evidence: No discussion on the scalability of SuperGen to larger datasets or computationally intensive tasks, important for practical deployment.

questions.question: How does SuperGen perform on NLU tasks outside classification?
questions.motivation: Understanding applicability to diverse NLU tasks enhances utility and generalizability.

questions.question: What are the specific criteria for quality in generated data?
questions.motivation: Clear criteria ensure generated data improves model training and performance.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Novelty in Approach. The use of synthetic training data is not new, with similar techniques previously applied using LSTM-based models. The paper should specify its unique aspects. Lines 92-93
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient Evaluation Metrics. The evaluation is limited to seven classification tasks from GLUE, restricting the scope of applicability. Lines 49-50
critique_points.point_003: Overemphasis on Tuning Hyperparameters. The reliance on fine-tuned hyperparameters like temperature and top-k suggests a need for extensive tuning, possibly reducing practical applicability. Lines 108-109
critique_points.point_004: Unclear Impact of Components on Performance. The contribution of each component like label smoothing or temporal ensembling lacks detailed evaluation through isolated analyses or ablation studies. Lines 52-54
critique_points.point_005: Limited Discussion of Task Suitability. The approach is pitched for classification problems but lacks discussion on its applicability to other task types. Lines 100-101
critique_points.point_006: Ambiguity in Data Quality Control Mechanisms. Criteria for selecting quality generated data remain vague without specified thresholds or methods. Lines 52-53
critique_points.point_007: Lack of Comprehensive Baseline Comparisons. The paper focuses on zero-shot prompting comparisons without addressing transfer learning or data augmentation baselines. Lines 49-51
critique_points.point_008: Potential Overclaiming of Results. The claim of performance equivalent to few-shot methods needs stronger evidence across a broader set of metrics. Lines 13-16
critique_points.point_009: Inadequate Discussion of Real-World Applicability. Discussion on potential biases in generated data and their implications in real-world settings is missing
critique_points.point_010: Reproducibility Challenges. The process for replicating key results, including text generation and selection, is underexplained, relying on supplementary material instead of the main text
critique_points.point_011: Lack of Task Diversity in Evaluation. Evaluation solely on classification tasks limits generalizability to other important NLU tasks like sequence labeling or QA
critique_points.point_012: Scalability to Larger Tasks or Corpuses. No discussion on the scalability of SuperGen to larger datasets or computationally intensive tasks, important for practical deployment
